subcategory: Clumping culm and edible shoot bamboo

planting.note: Plant divisions or container stock in spring in full sun in moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in zone 8–11; Punting Pole Bamboo is a medium-sized, non-invasive clumping bamboo native to Taiwan and southeastern China, producing dense clusters of straight, thin-walled, lightweight culms historically used as punting poles in Chinese rivers — and an excellent culinary bamboo with large, edible, sweet spring shoots.

directions: Plant in full sun in moist, fertile, well-drained soil; Bambusa tuldoides is a clumping bamboo that does not run — safe to plant in most garden settings without rhizome barriers; water deeply in establishment and regularly during shooting season for maximum edible shoot production. Bambusa tuldoides (Punting Pole Bamboo, Canton Bamboo, or Greenstripe Bamboo) is a medium to large, fast-growing, non-invasive clumping bamboo native to Taiwan and southeastern China (Guangdong, Fujian), widely cultivated across subtropical Asia and naturalized in many tropical regions. The culms are straight, thin-walled, and lightweight — traditionally harvested as punting poles for river transport in Guangdong and as construction material, scaffolding, and utility poles — yet flexible and strong. The spring shoots are among the most commercially important edible bamboo shoots in China, produced in large quantities as an important culinary ingredient. Bambusa tuldoides performs reliably as an ornamental landscape bamboo in zones 8–11, forming dense, attractive clumps 15–35 feet tall with glossy, dark-green foliage. Considerably smaller and more garden-appropriate than B. oldhamii while still producing excellent edible shoots. Excellent as privacy screen, windbreak, and edible bamboo grove plant for Gulf Coast and similar warm subtropical gardens.
sources: University of Florida IFAS — Bambusa tuldoides

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in partial shade to full sun in moist, fertile, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in zone 6–9; Anceps Bamboo is a moderate-sized, cold-hardy, clumping or slowly spreading bamboo native to the Himalayan foothills of northwestern India (Simla Hills), producing slender, arching culms and fine-textured foliage in graceful, informal clumps — among the most shade-tolerant of the Yushania bamboos and suitable for woodland and partial-shade garden settings.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; Yushania anceps spreads slowly by rhizomes but is not aggressively invasive — monitor spread and contain if desired with light trenching; excellent for woodland garden positions with filtered light. Yushania anceps (Anceps Bamboo or Indian Mountain Bamboo) is a medium-sized, clumping-to-slowly-spreading bamboo in the Yushania genus native to the temperate Himalayan foothills of northwestern India — particularly the Simla Hills (Himachal Pradesh) at elevations of 5,000–9,000 feet — where it grows in open montane forests, forest clearings, and hillside thickets. The culms are slender (0.5–1.5 inch diameter, 5–12 feet tall), gracefully arching, and clothed with numerous, fine, lance-shaped leaves that give the plant a delicate, soft-textured appearance. Yushania anceps occupies an intermediate growth category: more spreading than the strictly clumping Fargesia species but far less aggressive than most Phyllostachys runners — making it useful where a bamboo ground-cover effect is desired with controlled spread. It is among the more shade-tolerant of the Yushania group and performs well in light woodland or partial-shade garden positions. Hardier than many bamboos, tolerating temperatures well below zone 6 minimums when established in well-drained soil. An excellent, graceful bamboo for large-scale naturalistic or woodland garden settings.

planting.note: Plant divisions in fall or spring in partial shade to full sun in moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in zone 7–10; Square Bamboo is a unique ornamental running bamboo native to southern China and Japan, the only widely cultivated bamboo species in which the mature culms develop distinctively square (4-sided) cross-sections — making it a horticultural curiosity and conversation piece, prized in Japanese tea garden design for its unusual geometry.

directions: Plant with rhizome barrier in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; Chimonobambusa quadrangularis is a running bamboo — install deep HDPE rhizome barrier before planting to prevent spreading into neighboring areas; an interesting choice for Japanese garden or tea garden settings where the unusual square culms create visual interest. Chimonobambusa quadrangularis (Square Bamboo or Japanese Square Bamboo) is a distinctive, medium-sized running bamboo native to southern China (Fujian, Hunan, Guangdong) and long cultivated in Japanese gardens and tea garden design. The unusual and botanically remarkable feature is the development of distinctly 4-angled (square in cross-section) culms on mature plants — a property unique among commonly cultivated bamboos and caused by differential cell growth at the node internodes. Young shoots are round or only slightly angular; older culms develop the pronounced square shape that makes this bamboo instantly recognizable. The culms grow 4–15 feet tall, 0.5–1 inch in diameter, slender, and often sport decorative spine-like branch remnants at the nodes on mature culms. Chimonobambusa is a fall-shooting bamboo (unusual — most bamboos shoot in spring), producing its new culms September through November. It is moderately spreading (runner) and should be contained with rhizome barrier; the rhizomes are fairly superficial and relatively easy to manage by annual cutting. An outstanding and unusual addition to Japanese-style, contemplative, or tea garden designs.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun in very well-drained, lean, rocky to gravelly, neutral to alkaline soil in zone 7–11; Mexican Grass Tree is a striking, long-lived architectural accent plant native to the highlands of Nuevo Leon and Tamaulipas, Mexico — forming a huge, rounded, porcupine-like mass of extremely long, narrow, unarmed leaves (to 6 feet) radiating from a central stem that eventually develops into a tree trunk, used as a dramatic focal point in hot, dry Southwestern desert and xeriscape gardens.

directions: Plant in full sun in very well-drained, lean, rocky to gravelly, neutral to slightly alkaline soil; Dasylirion longissimum requires excellent drainage and minimal supplemental water once established — it is highly drought and heat tolerant but will rot in poorly drained, wet, or clay soils. Dasylirion longissimum (Mexican Grass Tree or Toton Palm) is a dramatic, slow-growing architectural succulent in the Asparagaceae family native to the semi-arid highland mountains of Nuevo Leon, Coahuila, and Tamaulipas in northeastern Mexico, where it grows on rocky limestone slopes and ridges in full sun. Unlike the spoon-leaf Dasylirion species (D. wheeleri, D. texanum) whose leaves have serrated or spiny edges, D. longissimum is distinguished by its extremely long (to 6 feet), narrow, strap-like, essentially unarmed and smooth-edged leaves that radiate from the growing center in an enormous, perfect sphere — giving the plant an appearance of a giant teal-green sea urchin or porcupine. Over many years and decades, the plant develops a raised, cylindrical trunk beneath the leaf mass, eventually reaching 10–20 feet in old age. The flower stalk when it finally appears is a spectacular 10–15 foot spike covered with thousands of tiny cream flowers. Excellent for Southwestern desert gardens, xeriscape plantings, and architectural accent positions in hot, dry, alkaline-soil landscapes in zones 7–11.
sources: University of Arizona Cooperative Extension — Dasylirion

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in zone 8–10; Mountain Papaya (Chamorro, Babaco relative) is a small, fast-growing tree native to the Andean highlands of Peru, Ecuador, Bolivia, and Colombia at 5,000–10,000 feet elevation — producing clusters of small, oblong, yellow-orange fruits with white papaya-like flesh and a sharp, acidic, fragrant flavor used in Latin American cooking and juices, while tolerating cool, even light-frost temperatures that kill common papaya.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil; Vasconcellea pubescens is remarkably cold-tolerant compared to common papaya (Carica papaya) — tolerating brief temperatures to 26–28°F when established, making it the best papaya-like fruit tree for zone 8 climates — water regularly, fertilize monthly with balanced fertilizer, and prune to maintain tidy form. Vasconcellea pubescens (Mountain Papaya, Chamorro, or Chihualcan Papaya) is a small to medium-sized, fast-growing, short-lived tree in the Caricaceae (papaya) family native to the Andean highlands of Ecuador, Peru, Bolivia, Chile, and Colombia at elevations of 1,500–3,200 meters, where the cool, moist, frost-prone mountain climate would immediately kill common papaya (Carica papaya). As one of the papaya family's most cold-hardy species, V. pubescens withstands light frosts that damage the common papaya — making it the most appropriate papaya relative for cultivation in mild-winter zone 8–10 climates across southern North America and the Mediterranean. The clusters of small (2–4 inch), oblong, yellow-orange, smooth-skinned fruits contain firm, white, strongly aromatic, papaya-like flesh with a distinctive, sharp, tropical-citrus-sour flavor — eaten raw, cooked in stews and soups (popular in Chilean cocina criolla), juiced, and made into jam and preserves across its Andean native range. Plants are typically dioecious and very fast to first fruit (1–2 years from seed), making them productive even as short-lived trees.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in partial shade to full sun in moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in zone 8–10; Tree Tomato (Tamarillo) is a fast-growing, small ornamental tree native to the Andean highlands of Peru, Ecuador, Bolivia, and Chile — bearing prolific clusters of egg-shaped, deep-red to orange-yellow, glossy, thin-skinned fruits with tangy-sweet, tomato-passionfruit-flavored flesh popular across Latin America, New Zealand, and increasingly in international markets.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil; Solanum betaceum grows fast but has brittle branches — provide wind protection and stake when young; water deeply and regularly and fertilize monthly for maximum fruiting; prune after fruiting to maintain shape and encourage new productive growth. Solanum betaceum (Tree Tomato or Tamarillo, syn. Cyphomandra betacea) is a fast-growing, soft-wooded, small tree in the Solanaceae (nightshade) family native to the semi-arid and sub-humid Andean highlands of Peru, Ecuador, Bolivia, Colombia, and northern Chile at elevations of 5,000–10,000 feet. The tree produces prolific clusters of glossy, egg-shaped, thin-skinned fruits — deep red, orange, or yellow depending on cultivar — with tangy-sweet, intensely flavored flesh combining tomato, passion fruit, and tropical notes, surrounding a central core of dark-purple seeds. Tamarillos are extensively eaten fresh (the skin is typically removed), made into juice, jams, chutneys, salsas, and cooked sauces across Ecuador, Peru, Colombia, and especially New Zealand, where the industry was developed from Andean introductions in the mid-20th century. Trees fruit prolifically in 1–2 years from transplants, providing remarkable returns for a tree-fruit crop. The plants are susceptible to tobacco mosaic virus, cold below 28°F, and water stress — they perform best in cool, frost-light subtropical conditions (zone 8b–10) with consistent moisture. Named a 'new world food' candidate for its high vitamin C, antioxidant, and nutritional content.
